children.name: Cathartes
children.description: A genus of three medium-sized vultures with highly developed olfactory senses, a rare adaptation among raptors that allows them to locate carrion by scent. Their naked heads and dark plumage are classic vulture traits, and they are among the most efficient scavengers in the Americas.

children.name: Coragyps
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the black vulture, a highly social and aggressive scavenger that often displaces other vultures at carcasses. Lacking a well-developed sense of smell, it relies on sight and follows turkey vultures to locate food.

children.name: Gymnogyps
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the California condor, one of the world's rarest and most celebrated birds. With a wingspan exceeding three metres, it is one of the largest flying birds and the subject of an intensive captive-breeding and reintroduction programme that saved it from the brink of extinction.

children.name: Vultur
children.description: A monotypic Andean genus containing the largest raptor in the world by wingspan. The Andean condor is a cultural icon across South America, representing power and freedom in Andean mythology and featuring prominently on the national coats of arms of several countries.

children.name: Sarcoramphus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the king vulture, the most colourful New World vulture with a brilliantly variegated head in shades of red, orange, yellow, and purple. Its powerful bill allows it to tear through tough hides that smaller vultures cannot penetrate.
